content.text: Real-world agent pipelines rarely consume pure JSON end-to-end. Configuration files arrive as YAML, analytics exports come as CSV, legacy enterprise APIs respond with XML, and streaming logs flow as newline-delimited JSON. Understanding how to parse, validate, and normalize each format — and when to convert versus consume natively — is a foundational skill for building reliable agentic systems.

Data serialization formats define how structured information is encoded as text or bytes for storage and transmission. Each format makes different trade-offs between human readability, machine efficiency, schema strictness, and ecosystem support. The four formats an agent developer encounters most frequently outside of JSON are YAML, CSV, XML, and JSONL.

(YAML Ain't Markup Language) uses indentation and minimal punctuation to represent nested data. It is the dominant format for configuration files — Docker Compose, Kubernetes manifests, GitHub Actions workflows, and most LLM framework config files are authored in YAML. Its human-friendliness and support for comments make it the natural choice when a developer needs to read and edit a file directly.

(Comma-Separated Values) encodes tabular data as plain text rows. Every spreadsheet, CRM export, analytics platform, and database dump can produce CSV. Agents frequently receive CSV from tool calls that query databases or call reporting APIs.

(Extensible Markup Language) wraps data in named tags with attributes. It dominated enterprise and government data exchange in the 2000s and still appears in SOAP web services, RSS/Atom feeds, Microsoft Office files, SVG graphics, and some financial APIs such as XBRL.

(JSON Lines / newline-delimited JSON) stores one valid JSON object per line. It is the format of choice for structured logs, training datasets, and streaming API responses, because each line can be processed independently without loading an entire array into memory.
(Tom's Obvious Minimal Language) fills a similar role to YAML for project configuration — it is the format of

An agent that can only speak JSON is blind to everything upstream of its immediate tool results. Deployment pipelines inject settings from YAML. Data analysis tasks hand off CSV exports from databases. CRM, ERP, and government data integrations still speak XML. Without reliable parsers at the agent boundary, the developer must manually transform data before it enters the pipeline — a fragile, maintenance-heavy approach that breaks every time an upstream system changes its output format.

More importantly, each format carries subtle correctness risks that only surface at scale: YAML's default loader can execute arbitrary Python code; CSV has no schema so every field arrives as a string; XML namespaces break naive element lookup; large CSV or XML files can silently exceed context windows when injected raw into prompts. Knowing how each format works — and where it fails — is what separates a brittle prototype from a production-ready agent.
: YAML lets developers write and review agent configuration without touching code. Comments, multi-line strings, and anchors make complex configs legible in version control.
: Every database, spreadsheet, and BI tool can produce and consume CSV, making it the lowest-friction format for data handoff between systems.
: XML attributes, namespaces, and XSD schemas enable detailed self-describing documents. SOAP APIs enforce strict contracts that catch integration errors early.
: Because each record is an independent line, JSONL files can be read, appended, and tail-followed without parsing the entire file, making them ideal for high-volume logs and ML datasets.
: TOML has a strict type system where integers, floats, booleans, and dates are distinct types, avoiding the YAML Norway problem where
: Consuming data in its native format avoids a lossy conversion step and preserves structure that conversion might collapse — for example, XML attributes versus element text.
: The YAML 1.1 spec contains over 80 pages of rules. Implicit type coercion turns

into booleans. Anchors and aliases can create circular references. The default PyYAML loader allows arbitrary Python object deserialization — a remote code execution risk if used with untrusted input.

: Every field is a string. Numeric types, dates, booleans, and nulls must be inferred or coerced explicitly. The delimiter, quoting character, and line ending are not specified in the file itself — they must be discovered or agreed out-of-band.

: Parsing XML with ElementTree silently handles namespace prefixes; real-world XML documents from SOAP APIs use namespaces heavily, requiring explicit handling. Large XML documents build an entire DOM in memory.

: Language models are trained on JSON and plain text far more than on YAML or XML. Feeding raw YAML or XML into a prompt increases token count and can degrade extraction accuracy compared to converting to a concise JSON or Markdown summary first.
: CSV files from Windows often use CP-1252 encoding; XML files declare their encoding in the prolog; YAML is always UTF-8 per spec. Ignoring encoding causes silent corruption on non-ASCII data.

Each format is parsed by a dedicated library into Python's native data structures (dicts, lists, strings, numbers). The parsing step is followed by validation — checking that required fields are present and that types match expectations — and finally by normalization, which converts the Python data into a JSON-serializable form suitable for tool results or prompt injection.
function parses a YAML stream into Python dicts, lists, strings, integers, floats, and booleans. It refuses to deserialize Python-specific tags like
library is an alternative that preserves comments and round-trips the file without altering formatting — useful when an agent needs to read, modify, and rewrite a config file.

import yaml

# Always safe_load — never yaml.load() without SafeLoader
with open("agent_config.yaml", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
    config = yaml.safe_load(fh)

# Typed access after loading
model_name: str = config["agent"]["model"]
tools: list[str] = config["agent"].get("tools", [])
max_turns: int = int(config["agent"].get("max_turns", 20))
, which yields each row as a dict keyed by the header row. Every value is a string; type conversion is the developer's responsibility. The
function auto-detects types and handles large files efficiently via chunked reading, but adds a significant import cost — prefer the stdlib module for lightweight agent tool wrappers.

import csv, json

rows: list[dict] = []
with open("leads.csv", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
    reader = csv.DictReader(fh)
    for row in reader:
        # Explicit coercion — csv gives everything as str
        rows.append({
            "id": int(row["id"]),
            "name": row["name"].strip(),
            "score": float(row["score"]) if row["score"] else None,
        })

# Summarize for model context — never dump the full file raw
preview = json.dumps(rows[:10], ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
module parses XML into a tree of
objects. Element tags include namespace prefixes in Clark notation, e.g.
third-party library handles large files faster via a C-accelerated SAX parser and supports XPath 1.0 queries. For untrusted XML, use
to block entity expansion attacks (billion laughs / XXE).

import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

# Parsing a SOAP-style response
xml_text = """
<catalog>
  <item id="1"><title>Widget A</title><price currency="USD">9.99</price></item>
  <item id="2"><title>Widget B</title><price currency="USD">14.99</price></item>
</catalog>
"""

root = ET.fromstring(xml_text)
items = []
for el in root.findall(".//item"):
    items.append({
        "id": el.get("id"),
        "title": el.findtext("title"),
        "price": float(el.findtext("price") or 0),
        "currency": el.find("price").get("currency"),
    })
# items => [{"id": "1", "title": "Widget A", "price": 9.99, "currency": "USD"}, ...]

JSONL files are read line-by-line. Each non-empty line is a complete JSON document. This makes JSONL suitable for files too large to fit in memory — the agent processes records incrementally. When generating JSONL output (e.g. logging tool calls), flush after each line so crashes do not corrupt the file.

The diagram below shows how data in various external formats flows through the agent's ingestion layer before reaching the LLM or tool execution core. Each format enters through its own parser, is validated and type-coerced, then normalized to a Python dict or list and optionally summarized before being injected into the prompt or returned as a tool result.

attrs.alt: Data format ingestion pipeline: YAML, CSV, XML, JSONL flow through parse, validate, normalize, and agent/LLM consumption stages

content.text: The critical design principle is that formats are translated at the boundary — the core agent logic never handles raw bytes or format-specific syntax. Validation (via Pydantic or explicit checks) sits between the parser output and the agent's internal data model, ensuring that malformed or unexpected inputs fail fast with a descriptive error rather than corrupting downstream tool calls.
: for agent and platform configuration that humans read and edit — model selection, tool lists, prompt templates, retry policies. Keep secrets out of YAML files committed to version control.
: when a tool call or API returns tabular data (CRM records, analytics exports, SQL query results). Read with
, coerce types, then either summarize for prompt context or pass a typed list of dicts to the next tool.
: only when integrating systems that mandate it — SOAP services, some government open-data APIs, RSS/Atom feeds. Convert to a minimal dict as early as possible and discard the raw XML.
: for structured logging of agent traces, tool call histories, and evaluation datasets. Prefer JSONL over a single large JSON array because the file remains valid after an incomplete write.
: for all intra-agent data exchange — tool arguments, tool results, API request bodies, and LLM messages. When in doubt, convert to JSON.
: YAML for secrets; raw CSV/XML in prompts without summarization; multi-megabyte XML documents without prior extraction.

A robust format-handling layer in an agent follows five steps: detect the format, parse it with the appropriate library, validate the result against a Pydantic model, normalize to JSON-serializable types, and limit size before injecting into context.
: Use the file extension or the MIME type from the HTTP response header. Fall back to content sniffing only as a last resort.
: Define a model for the expected shape. Pydantic v2 coerces types automatically and raises descriptive errors that the agent can surface to the user.
: Ensure all values are JSON-serializable — no bare

: Truncate large datasets to a representative sample, produce a Markdown table or plain-text summary, and expose pagination through the tool interface rather than dumping thousands of rows into a single prompt turn.
Example 1 — YAML config and Pydantic validation

# Demonstrates loading and validating a YAML agent config
import yaml
from pydantic import BaseModel

class AgentConfig(BaseModel):
    model: str
    max_turns: int = 20
    tools: list[str] = []

yaml_text = """
agent:
  model: claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
  max_turns: 30
  tools:
    - web_search
    - read_file
"""

raw = yaml.safe_load(yaml_text)
cfg = AgentConfig.model_validate(raw["agent"])

print(cfg.model)      # claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
print(cfg.max_turns)  # 30
print(cfg.tools)      # ['web_search', 'read_file']

# Attempt invalid model name
try:
    AgentConfig.model_validate({"model": "unknown-model"})
except Exception as e:
    print(e)  # validation error describing the constraint
Example 2 — CSV-to-Markdown summary for prompt context

import csv, io

SAMPLE_CSV = """id,name,revenue,churn_risk
1,Acme Corp,120000,low
2,Initech,45000,high
3,Umbrella Ltd,980000,medium
4,Globex,310000,low
"""

def csv_to_markdown(csv_text: str, max_rows: int = 10) -> str:
    """Convert CSV to a Markdown table suitable for LLM prompt injection."""
    reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(csv_text))
    rows = list(reader)[:max_rows]
    if not rows:
        return "(empty)"
    headers = list(rows[0].keys())
    sep = "| " + " | ".join("---" for _ in headers) + " |"
    header_row = "| " + " | ".join(headers) + " |"
    data_rows = [
        "| " + " | ".join(str(row.get(h, "")) for h in headers) + " |"
        for row in rows
    ]
    return "\n".join([header_row, sep] + data_rows)

print(csv_to_markdown(SAMPLE_CSV))
# | id | name | revenue | churn_risk |
# | --- | --- | --- | --- |
# | 1 | Acme Corp | 120000 | low |
# | 2 | Initech | 45000 | high |
# | 3 | Umbrella Ltd | 980000 | medium |
# | 4 | Globex | 310000 | low |
Example 3 — XML extraction from a namespaced SOAP response

import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import json

# Simulated SOAP envelope response with namespace
soap_xml = """<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<env:Envelope xmlns:env="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/"
              xmlns:ns="http://example.com/inventory">
  <env:Body>
    <ns:GetInventoryResponse>
      <ns:item sku="WGT-001" qty="150" price="9.99"/>
      <ns:item sku="WGT-002" qty="43"  price="19.99"/>
    </ns:GetInventoryResponse>
  </env:Body>
</env:Envelope>"""

root = ET.fromstring(soap_xml)
ns = {
    "env": "http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/",
    "ns":  "http://example.com/inventory",
}

items = [
    {
        "sku":   el.get("sku"),
        "qty":   int(el.get("qty", 0)),
        "price": float(el.get("price", 0)),
    }
    for el in root.findall(".//ns:item", ns)
]

print(json.dumps(items, indent=2))
# [
#   {"sku": "WGT-001", "qty": 150, "price": 9.99},
#   {"sku": "WGT-002", "qty": 43,  "price": 19.99}
# ]

Consider a sales intelligence agent that receives a weekly CRM export as CSV, reads its own behavioral config from a YAML file, logs every tool call to JSONL for observability, and must occasionally query a legacy procurement system that exposes a SOAP XML API.
to get its model, retry budget, and tool list. When the scheduled CSV arrives it parses the file with

, coerces numeric columns, and generates a Markdown table of the top 20 accounts by revenue to inject into the first user turn. For the procurement query it fires a SOAP request, deserializes the response with ElementTree, extracts the relevant fields, and returns a JSON list to the LLM. Every tool call and result is appended to
so the team can replay and debug without re-running the agent.
silently executes arbitrary Python constructors embedded in the YAML string. Any YAML sourced from user input, a network response, or an untrusted file can exploit this. Always use
every value is a string. Writing
Injecting raw CSV or XML into prompts
: A 5,000-row CSV at 100 characters per row consumes roughly 500,000 characters of context. Summarize, filter, or chunk the data; never dump the raw file into a single message.
: Files from Excel on Windows use CP-1252 by default. Open with
(to strip the BOM) or detect encoding with the
library. Incorrect encoding produces silent mojibake on names containing non-ASCII characters.
fails silently when the XML uses namespaces. The tag
in Clark notation. Always pass a namespace dict to
: API keys, database passwords, and tokens must come from environment variables or a secrets manager, never from a YAML file checked into version control.
Breaking a JSONL file with a partial write
: A crash mid-line leaves an incomplete JSON object on the last line. Always write the newline character after the full JSON string, and validate the tail of the file on startup.
; pin PyYAML >= 6.0 in dependencies to get the safe-by-default API.
Validate every parsed config with a Pydantic v2 model immediately after loading — fail at startup, not mid-run.
instead of stdlib ElementTree for any XML arriving from an external network source to block XXE and billion-laughs attacks.
Convert data to JSON or a concise Markdown table before injecting into prompts. Expose pagination via the tool interface rather than returning unbounded results.
Log all format-conversion errors with structured context (file path, line number, raw value) so debugging does not require re-running the agent.
Store secrets in environment variables or a secrets manager. Use
Write JSONL output with an explicit newline after each record and flush the buffer; this ensures each record is durable even if the process terminates unexpectedly.

For most agent use cases, data volumes are small enough that parser performance is not a bottleneck — a few thousand CSV rows, a Kubernetes YAML file, or a SOAP response with a few dozen records parses in milliseconds. The limiting factor is almost always the LLM's context window, not the parser speed.
to stream rows in chunks. Aggregate statistics (counts, sums, min/max) before surfacing results to the LLM; a one-sentence summary is more useful than 50,000 raw rows.
to process elements as they are parsed without loading the entire tree. Clear processed elements with
: Use a buffered writer and flush on a time or record-count threshold. For very high throughput consider a structured logging library (
) that handles rotation and async flushing.

: The main scalability concern is prompt size. A typical 4,096-token budget holds roughly 15,000 characters. A 100-row CSV with 10 columns easily exceeds this; a 10-row preview and aggregate stats almost never does. Always compute an approximate token count before injecting data.
: YAML parsing is CPU-intensive for very large files.
with the C extension is faster than pure-Python PyYAML. For immutable configs loaded once at startup the difference is negligible.
): The standard Python YAML library. Use
exclusively. Includes a C extension for speed.
): Round-trip parser that preserves comments and key order. Preferred when agents rewrite config files in place.
cover most use cases and correctly handle quoted fields with embedded commas and newlines.
with dtype inference, streaming chunks, and dozens of convenience methods. Heavyweight dependency — avoid in lightweight tool wrappers.
(stdlib): Sufficient for trusted XML. Fast C implementation in CPython. Does not support XPath 2.0 or XSLT.
): Fastest Python XML parser, full XPath 1.0, XSLT, and schema validation. Required for complex SOAP integrations.
): Drop-in replacement for stdlib XML modules that blocks all known XML attack vectors — XXE, billion laughs, quadratic blowup. Mandatory for any externally sourced XML.
(stdlib, Python 3.11+): Built-in TOML parser. For Python 3.10 and earlier use
): Merges environment variables, .env files, and YAML/TOML config into a validated Pydantic model. The canonical way to configure production agents.
The table below compares the data formats most commonly encountered in agentic AI pipelines across the dimensions that matter most at the integration boundary.
Primary wire format for tool I/O and API calls
Tabular data from tools and exports
All fields are strings; no nested data

Format handling is a security boundary. Malformed or adversarially crafted input can crash parsers, exhaust memory, or execute arbitrary code if the wrong library or API is used. Apply defense-in-depth: validate the format first, then validate the content, then limit the size before any processing.
YAML — remote code execution via unsafe loader
execute system commands when loaded with
raises an error without an explicit Loader.
in XML. When parsed, the entity resolves and leaks local files. Use
: Nested entity references expand exponentially, consuming all available memory.
can execute a spreadsheet formula when opened in Excel. Sanitize any cell that will be re-exported as CSV by rejecting the dangerous prefix or prepending a single quote.
: A tool that accepts arbitrary file paths or raw text should enforce a maximum size before parsing. A 1 GB XML file will exhaust memory even with a safe parser. Check
before opening, or limit streaming reads to a configured byte budget.
: YAML files committed to public repositories expose secrets to anyone with repository access. Scan git history with tools like
in CI to prevent accidental commits.
: Normalize all text to UTF-8 immediately after parsing. Non-UTF-8 byte sequences in field values can bypass downstream string-matching safety checks.
JSON is the primary wire format for agent tool I/O and LLM messages; YAML, CSV, XML, and JSONL appear at configuration and integration edges.
API allows arbitrary Python code execution in crafted YAML inputs.
Every field in a CSV row is a string after parsing; type coercion is explicit and mandatory. Use Pydantic models to automate this and catch type errors at startup rather than mid-run.
for any XML arriving from an external source; it blocks XXE injection, entity expansion attacks, and DTD-based denial-of-service with no API changes relative to stdlib.
Never inject raw CSV or XML into an LLM prompt. Convert to a concise Markdown table, a typed JSON array, or an aggregate summary and expose pagination through the tool interface.
JSONL is preferred over a single JSON array for logs and streaming datasets because each record is independent and the file stays valid after an interrupted write.
Validate every parsed config and data payload against a Pydantic model at the format boundary so that schema violations fail immediately with actionable error messages.
Keep secrets out of YAML config files; use environment variables or a secrets manager and merge them at runtime with
